body.text: 1. Theo had saved his allowance for three weeks to buy the red dragon kite in the shop window. On Saturday he raced to the park, eager to send it soaring into the bright blue sky.
2. The kite climbed higher and higher until a gust of wind tugged it sideways. Before Theo could pull the string, the kite snagged on the top branch of a tall oak tree and would not come loose.
3. Theo tugged and tugged, but the string only knotted tighter. His eyes stung with tears. He wanted to give up and walk home, leaving the kite behind.
4. Then he remembered the park ranger, Ms. Cole, who knew the park better than anyone. Theo took a deep breath and went to ask her for help instead of quitting.
5. Ms. Cole brought a long pole with a hook on the end. Together they gently lifted the string off the branch, and the red dragon floated back down to Theo's waiting hands.
6. Theo flew his kite the rest of the afternoon. He learned that asking for help is not the same as giving up.
1. Nina's family had moved across the state, and Monday was her first day at Pine Hill School. Her stomach felt like it was full of fluttering moths as she stood at the classroom door.
2. Everyone already seemed to have a friend. Nina slid into an empty seat in the back and stared at her shoes, sure that no one would talk to her.
3. At lunch she sat alone at the end of a long table. A girl named Priya noticed her and carried her tray over. "You can sit with us," Priya said with a smile.
4. Nina was too nervous to say much at first. But when Priya asked about her old town, the words began to spill out, and soon both girls were laughing.
5. By the end of the day, Priya had introduced Nina to three other classmates. Nina walked home feeling lighter, already looking forward to tomorrow.
6. Nina realized that a new place feels less scary when someone reaches out — and that she could be that someone for the next new student.
1. Owen was tossing a soft ball in the living room, even though his mom had asked him not to play ball indoors. On one wild throw, the ball knocked his grandmother's blue vase off the shelf.
2. The vase hit the floor and a long crack ran down its side. Owen's heart sank. The vase had belonged to his grandmother for many years.
3. For a moment Owen thought about hiding the vase behind the curtain and saying nothing. No one had seen what happened.
4. But the more he looked at the crack, the heavier the secret felt. When his mom came home, Owen took a deep breath and told her the truth.
5. His mom was not happy about the vase, but she hugged him. "Thank you for being honest," she said. "That matters more to me than the vase."
6. Together they glued the crack and set the vase back on the shelf. Owen felt the heavy feeling lift away, glad he had told the truth.
1. Maya wanted to run in the school's one-mile race, but she had never run that far before. The first time she tried, she had to stop and walk after just two laps.
2. "I'll never finish a whole mile," Maya sighed. Her older brother Sam shook his head. "You will," he said, "if you practice a little more each day."
3. So every afternoon Maya ran. On Monday she made it three laps. By Thursday she could run five without stopping. Her legs grew stronger week by week.
4. On race day, Maya's heart raced at the starting line. The whistle blew, and she settled into the steady pace she had practiced.
5. Her chest burned near the end, but she remembered all her hard work and kept going. Maya crossed the finish line, tired and proud.
6. She did not come in first, but she had run the whole mile without stopping. Maya saw that steady practice had carried her all the way to the finish.
1. One rainy morning, Caleb found a thin, shivering dog huddled by the garden gate. It had no collar and no tag, and it looked up at him with hopeful brown eyes.
2. Caleb wanted to keep the dog right away. But his dad reminded him that a pet is a big responsibility that lasts every single day, not just when it is fun.
3. To show he was ready, Caleb made a plan. He set an alarm to feed the dog each morning, walked it after school, and saved part of his allowance for dog food.
4. For two weeks Caleb kept his promise, even on cold days when he would rather have stayed inside. The dog, which he named Scout, grew round and happy.
5. Seeing how well Caleb cared for Scout, his parents agreed the dog could stay for good. Caleb beamed as Scout wagged its whole body with joy.
6. Caleb learned that taking care of someone means showing up every day, not just when you feel like it.
1. At the beach contest, Jada wanted to build the tallest sandcastle and win the blue ribbon all by herself. She picked a spot far from her cousin Leo and started digging.
2. Jada's tower kept crumbling. The dry sand near the top slid down as fast as she could pile it. Across the beach, Leo was having the same trouble.
3. "Want to work together?" Leo called. Jada almost said no — she wanted to win alone. But her castle was a lumpy heap, so she walked over to try his idea.
4. Together they learned that wet sand from the water's edge held its shape. They carried buckets of it and built strong, smooth walls that did not fall.
5. Their castle grew taller than anything either of them had made alone. It had towers, a bridge, and a deep moat that sparkled in the sun.
6. They did not win the blue ribbon, but Jada hardly minded. She had more fun building with Leo, and discovered that teamwork built something better than working alone.
1. Lia loved the library, and her favorite book of all was a thick one about deep-sea creatures. She checked it out so often that she felt it was almost hers.
2. One week, Lia could not find the book anywhere. It was not in her backpack, her desk, or under her bed. The due date was creeping closer.
3. Lia felt a flutter of worry. She thought about just staying away from the library so no one would ask about the missing book.
4. Instead, she went to Mr. Park, the librarian, and explained what had happened. "Thank you for telling me," he said kindly. "Let's figure it out together."
5. They searched the return cart and found the book — Lia had dropped it there by mistake the week before. The mystery was solved, and nothing was lost after all.
6. Lia felt proud that she had spoken up instead of hiding. She learned that facing a problem honestly is better than running from it.
1. Sam had practiced his piano song for the school talent show until he could play it with his eyes closed. But the thought of all those eyes watching made his hands turn cold.
2. Backstage, Sam peeked at the crowded gym and nearly turned to run home. "I can't do it," he whispered to his teacher, Mrs. Lin.
3. Mrs. Lin knelt beside him. "Being brave doesn't mean you aren't scared," she said. "It means you go on stage even though you are."
4. Sam took a shaky breath and walked to the piano. His first notes trembled, but as the song went on, his fingers remembered the way and the music flowed.
5. When the last note faded, the gym filled with clapping. Sam grinned, surprised at how glad he was that he had not run away.
6. Sam learned that courage is not the absence of fear, but doing the thing that scares you anyway.
1. Rosa helped her grandpa plant sunflower seeds in the spring. The very next day she ran outside, certain that tall flowers would already be waving in the breeze.
2. But the soil was bare. "Where are my sunflowers?" Rosa asked, frowning at the empty dirt. Grandpa chuckled and said good things take time.
3. Each morning Rosa checked the garden, and each morning the dirt looked the same. She wanted to dig up the seeds to see what was wrong.
4. "Let them be," Grandpa said gently. "The roots are growing where you cannot see." So Rosa kept watering and waiting, even when it was hard.
5. One day a tiny green shoot poked through the soil. Week by week it stretched up, until a sunflower taller than Rosa nodded its golden head.
6. Rosa learned that some good things are worth waiting for, even when nothing seems to be happening.
1. After the first big snow, Ben and his little sister Ellie planned to build a snow fort together on Saturday morning. Ben promised they would do it side by side.
2. But on Saturday, Ben's friends called to go sledding on the big hill. Ben grabbed his sled and was halfway out the door when he saw Ellie waiting in her snow boots.
3. Her face fell, though she tried to smile. "It's okay," she said quietly. "You can go." But Ben remembered the promise he had made.
4. He set down his sled. "I'll go sledding later," he told his friends. "I promised my sister we'd build a fort." Ellie's eyes lit up like candles.
5. Together they rolled giant snowballs and stacked them into thick walls. They packed a snow bench inside and waved tiny flags from the top.
6. Ben had fun he would have missed if he had left. He learned that keeping a promise to someone you love is worth more than any sledding hill.
1. Honeybees may be small, but they do some of the most important work in nature. Without them, many of the foods we love would be much harder to grow.

2. As a honeybee drinks sweet nectar from a flower, yellow dust called pollen sticks to its fuzzy body. When the bee visits the next flower, some pollen rubs off. This is called pollination, and it helps plants make seeds and fruit.
3. Bees also turn nectar into honey. Back at the hive, they store the nectar in tiny wax cups and fan it with their wings until it thickens into sweet honey for the winter.
4. A hive works as a team. Thousands of bees share the jobs of gathering nectar, guarding the hive, and caring for the young. Each bee has a part to play.
5. People can help honeybees by planting flowers and by not using harmful sprays. When we protect bees, they keep helping our gardens grow.
1. A rainbow is one of the most beautiful sights in the sky. It seems like magic, but a rainbow forms because of sunlight and water working together.
2. Sunlight looks white, but it is really made of many colors mixed together. You usually cannot see these separate colors.
3. When sunlight shines through tiny raindrops, each drop bends the light and splits it apart. The hidden colors spread out into a band you can see.
4. The colors always line up in the same order: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and violet. Red sits on the top of the arc and violet on the bottom.
5. That is why rainbows often appear right after a rain shower while the sun is still shining. You need both the sun and the raindrops to make one.
1. When you look at a plant, you see leaves and flowers above the ground. But some of a plant's most important parts are hidden below your feet: the roots.
2. Roots act like straws. They soak up water and tiny bits of food called nutrients from the soil and carry them up into the plant.
3. Roots also hold a plant in place. They spread out and grip the soil so that strong winds and heavy rain cannot easily knock the plant over.
4. Some roots do an extra job. Plants like carrots and beets store food inside their thick roots, saving energy for later. The part we eat is the root itself.
5. Roots may be out of sight, but they keep a plant fed, watered, and standing tall. A healthy plant always begins with healthy roots.
1. The octopus is one of the smartest animals in the ocean. It has eight arms, three hearts, and some surprising tricks for staying safe.
2. An octopus can change the color of its skin in less than a second. By matching the rocks and sand around it, the octopus hides from hungry sharks and eels.
3. If an enemy gets too close, the octopus has another trick. It squirts a cloud of dark ink into the water, then jets away while the enemy is confused.
4. Octopuses are also great problem solvers. In tests, they have learned to open jars and even sneak out of tanks to find food.
5. With its clever brain and clever body, the octopus is well prepared for life in the sea. There is far more to this animal than eight wiggly arms.
1. Every day, people throw away mountains of trash. But much of what we toss out does not have to be wasted. Recycling gives old things a brand-new life.
2. When you recycle paper, it can be turned into fresh paper instead of being buried in a landfill. This saves trees, because fewer need to be cut down.
3. Old cans and bottles can be melted and made into new ones. Recycling metal and glass uses far less energy than making them from scratch.
4. Recycling also keeps trash out of landfills, which are huge piles of garbage. Less trash means cleaner land, air, and water for everyone.
5. You can help by sorting paper, cans, and bottles into the right bins. Small choices add up to a big difference for the planet.
1. Each fall, you might notice flocks of birds flying south across the sky. Many kinds of birds travel long distances when the seasons change. This long trip is called migration.
2. Birds migrate mainly to find food. When winter comes, insects disappear and seeds get buried under snow, so birds fly to warmer places where food is easy to find.
3. Some birds travel amazing distances. The tiny Arctic tern flies from the top of the world to the bottom and back each year, the longest journey of any bird.
4. How do birds know the way? They use the sun, the stars, and even the Earth's magnetic pull to find their path, like a built-in map.
5. When spring returns and food is plentiful again, the birds fly back home to raise their young. Migration helps birds survive all year long.
